claim: The Currier A and B sub-corpora use two distinct cipher alphabets encoding the same underlying Latin plaintext, such that glyph-frequency distributions are statistically different between A and B but word-length distributions and entropy levels are statistically indistinguishable.
evidence_for: Currier A (11,022 words) and B (23,766 words) show documented glyph-level differences attributable to different cipher keys
High-confidence belief [0.677] that Currier A/B represents two scribes encoding the same language
If same plaintext language, word-length distributions should converge even when glyph distributions diverge
evidence_against: Belief [0.339] at moderate confidence that A/B encodes two different plaintext languages (e.g., Latin vs. Italian)
Different entropy levels between sections straddling the A/B split could indicate different underlying languages rather than different keys
Hapax ratio stability across A and B is unknown and could reveal divergent vocabularies
test: Compute word-length frequency distributions and per-section Shannon entropy separately for all Currier-A folios and all Currier-B folios. Run a two-sample Kolmogorov-Smirnov test on the word-length distributions and compare entropy difference with a bootstrap confidence interval.
test_metric: KS statistic D for word-length distributions A vs B; |H_A - H_B| in bits
test_threshold: KS p-value > 0.05 (distributions indistinguishable) AND |H_A - H_B| < 0.05 bits would support same-language-different-key; p-value < 0.01 OR |H_A - H_B| > 0.1 bits would support different plaintext languages

tests_run.test: compute word-length frequency distributions and per-section shannon entropy separately for all curri
tests_run.details: Observed glyph entropy: 3.8627 bits. Expected for hypothesis: ~4.0 bits. Difference: 0.1373 bits.

reasoning: This hypothesis is highly discriminating: it directly separates the two highest-weight competing explanations for the A/B split and requires only corpus statistics already partially computed
